section.title: Noah and the Ark
section.sectionRationale: This is the account of Noah. Against a corrupt and violent earth, one righteous man is introduced, given a plan of rescue, and drawn into the first covenant in Scripture. The unit runs from the heading, this is the account of Noah, to the closing note of his total obedience, framing the whole preparation for the flood.
section.thesis: In a world ripe for judgment God commits Himself by covenant to rescue, giving Noah an ark of salvation, and Noah answers with complete obedience.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Noah the righteous man in a corrupt world; the command to build the ark; the covenant and the gathering of life; and Noah's exact obedience.

units.label: A righteous man in a corrupt world

verses.text: This is the account of Noah. Noah was a {{righteous|righteous}} man, {{blameless|blameless}} in his generation; Noah walked with God.
And Noah had three sons: Shem, Ham, and Japheth.
Now the earth was corrupt in the sight of God, and full of violence.
And God looked upon the earth and saw that it was corrupt; for all living creatures on the earth had corrupted their ways.

units.label: The command to build the ark

verses.text: Then God said to Noah, “The end of all living creatures has come before Me, because through them the earth is full of violence. Now behold, I will destroy both them and the earth.
Make for yourself an {{ark|ark}} of gopher wood; make rooms in the ark and coat it with {{pitch|pitch}} inside and out.
And this is how you are to build it: The ark is to be 300 cubits long, 50 cubits wide, and 30 cubits high.
You are to make a roof for the ark, finish its walls a cubit from the top, place a door in the side of the ark, and build lower, middle, and upper decks.

units.label: The covenant and the gathering of life

verses.text: And behold, I will bring floodwaters upon the earth to destroy every creature under the heavens that has the breath of life. Everything on the earth will perish.
But I will establish My {{covenant|covenant}} with you, and you will enter the ark, you and your sons and your wife and your sons’ wives with you.
And you are to bring two of every living creature into the ark, male and female, to keep them alive with you.
Two of every kind of bird and animal and crawling creature will come to you to be kept alive.
You are also to take for yourself every kind of food that is eaten and gather it as food for yourselves and for the animals.”
So Noah did everything precisely as God had commanded him.

righteous.gloss: Righteous, just, in the right. Noah is the first person in the Bible called tsaddiq. Hebrews 11:7 explains that he became an heir of the righteousness that comes by faith, so his righteousness is real yet received, not a claim of sinless perfection.

blameless.gloss: Blameless, whole, of integrity; the word later used for sacrificial animals without defect. Noah was blameless in his generation, that is, a man of tested integrity who stood out in a corrupt age, not a man wholly without sin.

ark.gloss: A chest or vessel, a box for floating. The word is used only twice in the Bible, here for Noah's ark and later for the basket that carries baby Moses through the waters. Both are vessels of rescue that bear the chosen through deadly waters into a new life.

pitch.gloss: The tar or bitumen that sealed the ark; the noun is closely tied to the verb kaphar, to cover or make atonement, and to kopher, a ransom price. The covering that keeps the waters of judgment out of the ark quietly pictures the covering that shields God's people from wrath.

covenant.gloss: A covenant, a solemn binding commitment. This is the first time the word appears in Scripture. God establishes His covenant with Noah, binding Himself to rescue; the covenant of grace, God committing to save a people, begins to take visible shape here and runs on to its fulfillment in Christ.

basic.context: The world is corrupt and violent, but one man, Noah, is righteous and walks with God. God tells Noah that judgment is coming and gives him a plan: build a huge boat, an ark, to save his family and the animals. God also promises to make a covenant with Noah. The question is whether Noah will trust and obey.
basic.hermeneutics: This really happened, and it shows God saving through the ark. A key question is what it means that Noah was righteous and blameless. It does not mean he never sinned; the Bible says he was made right by faith. The point is that God graciously provides a way of rescue before the judgment falls.
basic.originalLanguages: Noah is the first man called righteous, tsaddiq, and blameless, tamim, a word later used for spotless sacrifices. He builds an ark, a tebah, the same word used for baby Moses' basket. He seals it with pitch, kopher, a word tied to covering and atonement. And God establishes His covenant, berit, the first covenant in the Bible.
basic.typology: The ark is a picture of salvation: those inside are carried safely through the waters of judgment, as we are carried safely in Christ. The pitch that covers the ark hints at the covering of atonement. And the one door in the ark's side points to Christ, the one door into safety.
basic.christ: The ark that saves through the flood points to Jesus, the true refuge who carries His people safely through judgment. Noah's righteousness by faith is the same righteousness we receive in Christ. And God's covenant to save, begun here, is fulfilled in the new covenant in Jesus' blood.
basic.god: God is just, He will not ignore a world drowning in violence. But He is also gracious, He warns Noah, provides an ark, and binds Himself by covenant to rescue. Judgment and salvation come from the same God, and He commits Himself to save before the storm arrives.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 11 says Noah built the ark by faith and became heir of righteousness by faith. 1 Peter 3 links the ark's rescue through water to salvation. John 10 shows Christ as the door. Genesis 6:18 is the Bible's first covenant.
basic.oneStory: In a world ready for judgment, God commits Himself to rescue. He gives Noah an ark and the first covenant of Scripture, and Noah obeys completely. This vessel of salvation and this promise to save press forward through the whole Bible toward Christ, the true ark and the mediator of a better covenant.

context.literary: A new toledot section, this is the account of Noah, sets the frame. Repeated words for corrupt and violence hammer the world's ruin, then God's detailed instructions and the first mention of covenant turn the account toward rescue, closing with the seal of Noah's obedience.
context.historical: The dimensions, materials, and design reflect a real, seaworthy barge of enormous size. For Israel, this account grounded the flood as history and showed God graciously providing a means of salvation amid deserved judgment.
context.canonical: The first covenant in the Bible and the first great picture of salvation through judgment. The New Testament reads the ark as a type of the salvation found in Christ and of baptism, 1 Peter 3:20-21.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To introduce Noah as the righteous man through whom God will save, to record God's covenant commitment and detailed provision of the ark, and to commend Noah's complete obedience.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The building instructions were prescriptive for Noah alone; for us the passage prescribes the faith and obedience it models, and describes the God who saves through judgment.

debates.question: In what sense was Noah righteous and blameless?

views.label: Righteous by his own godly character
views.case: The text plainly calls Noah righteous and blameless in his generation and says he walked with God, presenting him as a genuinely upright man who stood against the corruption around him.

views.label: Righteous by grace received through faith
views.case: Hebrews 11:7 says Noah became an heir of the righteousness that comes by faith, and chapter 6 first says he found grace before naming him righteous, so his uprightness flows from God's grace, not sinless merit; his later drunkenness confirms he was no sinless man.

debates.bearing: The two need not be opposed: Noah was truly godly, yet his righteousness was itself a gift of grace received by faith, the same pattern by which any sinner is counted righteous.

study.originalLanguages: The passage is threaded with weighty words. Noah is the Bible's first tsaddiq, righteous man, and is called tamim, blameless, the word later reserved for sacrifices without defect, marking his tested integrity in a rotten age. God commands a tebah, an ark, a word used only here and for the basket that saves baby Moses, both vessels bearing the chosen through deadly waters. Noah seals it with kopher, pitch, from the same root as kaphar, to cover or atone; the covering that holds back the waters of wrath whispers of atonement. And God speaks of His berit, covenant, for the first time in Scripture, binding Himself to save. Word by word, the vocabulary of rescue and atonement is being laid down.

typology.type: The ark bearing Noah through the flood
typology.fulfillment: Christ, in whom God's people are carried safely through judgment; the ark also prefigures baptism, 1 Peter 3:20-21.

typology.type: The pitch, kopher, covering the ark
typology.fulfillment: The atoning covering of Christ that shields believers from the waters of wrath, tied to the language of kaphar, to atone.

typology.type: The single door in the ark's side
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the one door through which any who would be saved must enter, John 10:9.

study.christ: The ark is one of Scripture's clearest pictures of salvation, and it points straight to Jesus. Those who enter the ark pass through the waters of judgment untouched, just as those who are in Christ pass through God's judgment safe; 1 Peter 3 makes the link explicit, tying the ark to baptism and to salvation. Noah's righteousness, received by faith according to Hebrews 11:7, is the very righteousness God credits to sinners in Christ. The pitch that covers the ark echoes the word for atonement, and the ark's single door anticipates Jesus who says, I am the door. Above all, the covenant God establishes here, His binding promise to save, opens the covenant story that reaches its climax in the new covenant sealed by Christ's blood.
study.god: God is both just and gracious. He will not overlook a world filled with violence and corruption; the flood is righteous judgment. Yet the same God who judges is the God who saves. Before a drop of rain falls, He warns Noah, gives a detailed plan of rescue, and, most strikingly, establishes His covenant, binding Himself by solemn promise to bring Noah and his family safely through. God takes the initiative in salvation; He commits Himself to it. And He is the God who is pleased by faith that obeys, as Noah did everything precisely as commanded.

crossReferences.note: By faith Noah, warned about things not yet seen, built an ark and became heir of the righteousness that comes by faith.
In the ark a few were saved through water, which prefigures the baptism that now saves through the resurrection of Christ.
I am the door; whoever enters through Me will be saved, echoing the one door of the ark.
The covenant first announced here is formally established after the flood with the sign of the rainbow.

study.oneStory: A corrupt and violent world stands under judgment, but God moves to save. He raises up a righteous man, gives him an ark, and speaks for the first time of covenant, binding Himself to rescue. Noah obeys completely, and the vessel of salvation takes shape. This ark and this covenant are early drafts of the whole gospel, carried forward through Scripture to Christ, the true refuge who brings His people through judgment into new creation.

quiz.q: How is Noah described against the world around him?

options.text: As a righteous man, blameless in his generation, who walked with God in a corrupt and violent world.
options.note: Noah's integrity stands out sharply against the surrounding corruption.

options.text: As the strongest and wealthiest man of his age.
options.note: The text highlights his righteousness and walk with God, not strength or wealth.

options.text: As no different from anyone else of his time.
options.note: The whole point is that Noah stood apart from a corrupt generation.

options.text: As a man who had never done anything wrong in his life.
options.note: Blameless means integrity, not sinless perfection; Noah later sins, and his righteousness is by faith.

quiz.q: What is significant about God establishing His covenant with Noah?

options.text: It is the first covenant in the Bible, God binding Himself by promise to rescue.
options.note: The word covenant appears here for the first time in Scripture.

options.text: It was a deal in which Noah earned his rescue by good works.
options.note: The covenant is God's gracious commitment, not payment for Noah's works.

options.text: It was only a private feeling with no real promise.
options.note: A covenant is a solemn binding commitment, not a vague feeling.

options.text: It cancelled the coming judgment entirely.
options.note: The judgment still comes; the covenant secures Noah's rescue through it.

quiz.q: How does the ark point to Christ?

options.text: It carries those inside safely through the waters of judgment, as Christ carries His people through judgment.
options.note: 1 Peter 3 links the ark's rescue to salvation in Christ.

options.text: It shows that good engineering can save us from anything.
options.note: The ark's meaning is theological rescue, not a lesson in engineering.

options.text: It teaches that only large families are saved.
options.note: The ark pictures salvation for those God brings in, not a rule about family size.

options.text: The ark has no link to Christ.
options.note: The New Testament reads the ark as a pattern of salvation in Christ.

quiz.q: What does the word for the pitch that sealed the ark hint at?

options.text: It shares a root with the word for atonement, hinting at a covering that shields from wrath.
options.note: Kopher, pitch, is tied to kaphar, to cover or atone.

options.text: It simply shows Noah was a skilled builder.
options.note: Skill is involved, but the word carries the deeper note of covering and atonement.

options.text: It means the ark was decorated beautifully.
options.note: The pitch was a waterproof seal, not decoration, and points to covering.

options.text: It has no deeper meaning at all.
options.note: The word's link to atonement gives it real theological weight.

quiz.q: How does Noah respond to God's instructions?

options.text: He did everything precisely as God had commanded him.
options.note: Noah's complete obedience is the closing note of the passage.

options.text: He obeyed only the parts that seemed reasonable to him.
options.note: The text stresses that he did everything, not just the convenient parts.

options.text: He argued with God about the plan first.
options.note: There is no argument recorded; Noah simply obeyed.

options.text: He delayed for years before starting.
options.note: The passage presents his obedience as full and faithful.

questions: Noah was called to live righteously while the whole world around him was corrupt. Where is God asking you to walk differently from the people and pressures around you right now?
God provided one ark, one way of rescue, before the judgment came. How does it comfort you to know that God provides the way of salvation rather than leaving us to save ourselves?
The passage says Noah did everything precisely as God commanded, even a huge task with no visible flood yet in sight. Where is God asking you to obey before you can see the reason why?
God bound Himself by covenant to rescue Noah. How does resting on God's promises, rather than your own performance, change the way you face an uncertain future?
The ark had a single door, and Jesus says He is the door to salvation. Have you entered that door yourself, and who around you still needs to hear the way in?

translationNotes.note: Gopher wood names an unknown timber, possibly cypress or cedar; the word for the pitch used to coat it is closely related to the Hebrew word for atonement, a link many see as more than coincidental.

Blameless in his generation renders a word meaning whole or of integrity, later used of unblemished sacrifices; it marks moral integrity and standing out in a corrupt age rather than sinless perfection.
